revision.text:
'''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|ja|イマヌエル・カント}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)}} * {{ill|Apperception|ja|統覚}} * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント}}
{{Aimai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Naikan}} [[Category:Unsorted kk]][[Category:unsorted disambiguation pages]]

'''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|ja|イマヌエル・カント}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)}} * {{ill|Apperception|ja|統覚}} * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント}}
{{Aimai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Naikan}} [[Category:Unsorted kk]][[Category:unsorted disambiguation pages]]

These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. revision.comment: Text replacement - "|外官}}" to "|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}}"
revision.text:
'''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|ja|イマヌエル・カント}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)}} * {{ill|Apperception|ja|統覚}} * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント}}
{{Aimai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Naikan}} [[Category:unsorted disambiguation pages]]

'''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Imperial Household Ministry|ja|宮内省}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|ja|イマヌエル・カント}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)}} * {{ill|Apperception|ja|統覚}} * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内官]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q114348108]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokan|qq=|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883145}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekan|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Imperial Household Ministry]] and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eunuchs|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=eunuch official|WD=Q24841818}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 感覚器|1=sensory nervous system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11101}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. [[:en:Immanuel Kant|Immanuel Kant]] stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Common sense|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共通感覚論|1=Common sense (book)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392785}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|qq=}} * [[:en:Apperception|Apperception]] * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philosophical concept of senses|qq=|1=sense|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173253}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Time (philosophy)|qq=|1=time|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11471}}
{{disambiguation}}
{{translated page|ja|内官|version=91901504|comment=Imported full ja history}}

While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. [[:en:Immanuel Kant|Immanuel Kant]] stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).

* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokan|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883145}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekan|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Imperial Household Ministry]] and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eunuchs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=eunuch official|WD=Q24841818}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 感覚器|1=sensory nervous system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11101}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内官]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q114348108]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokan|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883145}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekan|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Imperial Household Ministry]] and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eunuchs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=eunuch official|WD=Q24841818}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 感覚器|1=sensory nervous system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11101}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. [[:en:Immanuel Kant|Immanuel Kant]] stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Common sense|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共通感覚論|1=Common sense (book)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392785}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|qq=}} * [[:en:Apperception|Apperception]] * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philosophical concept of senses|1=sense|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173253}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Time (philosophy)|1=time|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11471}}
{{disambiguation}}

In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 感覚器|1=sensory nervous system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11101}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|en|Immanuel Kant|WD=Q9312|lt=Immanuel Kant}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Common sense|ja|共通感覚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Common sense|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共通感覚論|1=Common sense (book)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392785}} / {{ill|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|de|Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gemeinsinn (innerer Sinn)|qq=}} * {{ill|Apperception|en|Apperception|WD=Q620995|lt=Apperception}} * {{ill|Philosophical concept of senses|ja|感覚#哲学における感覚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philosophical concept of senses|1=sense|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173253}} * {{ill|Time (philosophy)|ja|時間#カント|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Time (philosophy)|1=time|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11471}}

'''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}}, it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokan|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883145}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekan|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Ministry}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eunuchs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=eunuch official|WD=Q24841818}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 感覚器|1=sensory nervous system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11101}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|en|Immanuel Kant|WD=Q9312|lt=Immanuel Kant}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内官]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内官]]
'''Naikan''' (内官)
* '''Naikan''' - In the Japanese {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, it refers to {{ill|Kyokan|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyokan|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10883145}} (capital officials) who are in contrast to {{ill|Gekan|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekan|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}} (provincial officials). These were officials and offices based in the capital. * '''Naikan''' - Officials serving the imperial court. This term was used for officials working in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qid=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Ministry|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}} and other offices related to the imperial family under the old system. Known as imperial household officials (宮内官). * '''Naikan''' - In Chinese, it refers to {{ill|eunuchs|ja|宦官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eunuchs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Eunuch official|qid=Q24841818}} or female attendants in the imperial court. * '''Naikan''' - The Japanese translation of the German term *innerer Sinn* (inner sense), also referred to as 内感. It means internal sense, internal sensory perception, or internal awareness. In philosophy, {{ill|Kankan|ja|感官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kankan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 感覚器|1=Sensory nervous system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11101}} (*Sinn* in German) encompasses both sensory organs and their perceptual functions, considering physiological and psychological functions in a unified manner. While sensory organs related to external perception are called *Gekan* (external sense), the ability to perceive internal consciousness is called *Naikan*. {{ill|Immanuel Kant|en|Immanuel Kant|qid=Q9312|lt=Immanuel Kant|1=Immanuel Kant}} stated, "Time is nothing but the form of inner sense" in contrast to external phenomena related to space such as shape or position (from *Critique of Pure Reason*, A33/B49). Additionally, in the second edition of *Critique of Pure Reason*, particularly in §.24 "On the Application of Categories to the Objects of the Senses in General" (B150-157), he revisits this concept in connection with self-affection (*Affektion* in German).
